sectorContext: State-controlled oil & gas major; sanctioned entity; strategic energy infrastructure with OT/ICS and global trading operations.
verdict: Rosneft is a top-tier target for both Western state-aligned disruptive actors and financially-motivated ransomware crews. As a sanctioned Russian energy champion it draws hacktivist and state-aligned hostility, while its exposed edge (VPN, RDP) invites eCrime. The dominant near-term risk is extortion or wiper operations against internet-facing infrastructure — one CRITICAL and two HIGH actors rate above the action threshold.

exposureMap.why: Symbolic + strategic value; disruption serves geopolitical signalling

exposureMap.driver: Critical OT / ICS in refining & pipelines
exposureMap.why: Physical-impact capability is a strategic deterrent asset
High willingness/ability to pay; downtime is extremely costly
Low-cost initial access into a high-value environment

actors.title: Sandworm / APT44
what: Premier state OT/ICS attack unit; author of Industroyer & NotPetya
Specialises in destructive wiper and grid-disruption operations
Blends espionage, pre-positioning and physical-impact tradecraft
actors.why: As Russia's own energy champion, Rosneft is less a target than a potential proxy/collateral in intra-state operations — but the same TTPs (OT wipers, supply-chain implants) define the ceiling of what a hostile actor could do to comparable infrastructure.

rem.title: OT segmentation review
rem.body: Enforce IEC 62443 zones; isolate ICS from IT VPN reach.

rem.title: Managed NDR for OT
rem.body: 24/7 detection tuned to ICS protocols and wiper precursors.

rem.title: OT incident retainer
rem.body: Colt PSF wiper-recovery & forensic readiness playbook.

actors.title: APT28 / Fancy Bear
what: Long-running GRU espionage set targeting energy & government
Heavy use of credential phishing and edge-device exploitation
actors.why: APT28's edge-focused access model maps directly onto Rosneft's exposed VPN/webmail surface. Even as intra-state espionage, credential harvesting here feeds broader strategic collection and could enable third-party access.

rem.title: Patch edge devices
rem.body: Prioritise VPN, webmail and router firmware (KEV catalog).

rem.title: SASE + MFA
rem.body: Colt SASE terminates exposed VPN; enforce phishing-resistant MFA.

rem.title: NTLM hardening
rem.body: Block outbound SMB; deploy free NTLM-relay mitigations.

actors.title: Qilin (Agenda)
what: Prolific RaaS crew; Rust/Go lockers with fast encryption
Primary access vector: exposed/unpatched VPN and RDP
evidence: Initial access via CVE-2026-50751 VPN RCE
actors.why: This is the near-term worst case. Rosneft's exposed VPN gateway (CVE-2026-50751, finding C-01) is exactly Qilin's preferred entry. A successful intrusion would combine trading-floor downtime with data-leak extortion — the highest expected-loss scenario in the C-BIQ model.
actors.refs: Colt Shodan finding C-01; Qilin leak-site telemetry

rem.title: Emergency VPN patch
rem.body: Remediate CVE-2026-50751 immediately; rotate all VPN creds.

rem.title: Managed NDR + DDoS
rem.body: Detect lateral movement & encryption staging in real time.

rem.title: Ransomware retainer
rem.body: Colt PSF containment, negotiation-support and recovery.

actors.title: NoName057(16)
what: Targets perceived adversaries of Russian interests
High-volume but generally low-sophistication L7 floods
evidence: Peaks > 1 Tbps observed on some targets
actors.why: Ambiguous alignment: as a Russian entity Rosneft is unlikely a primary target, but mistaken-identity or spill-over floods against its public portals remain plausible. Impact is availability-only, but customer-facing outages carry reputational cost.

rem.title: Managed DDoS
rem.body: Always-on Colt DDoS scrubbing for public-facing assets.

rem.title: CDN / WAF
rem.body: Absorb L7 floods; rate-limit and geo-fence portals.

rem.title: Portal hardening
rem.body: Cache static content; free WAF rules for volumetric L7.

actors.title: Anonymous / IT Army
what: Loose banner for pro-Ukraine / anti-Russia operations
Data-leak, defacement and doxxing of Russian entities
Low coordination; opportunistic exploitation of exposed assets
evidence: IT Army of Ukraine tasking channels
actors.why: As a flagship sanctioned Russian company, Rosneft is a symbolic target for this banner. Capability is low and diffuse, but any exposed asset or leaked credential set could be seized opportunistically for a reputational hit.

rem.title: Credential hygiene
rem.body: Monitor breach dumps; force resets on exposed accounts.

rem.title: Attack-surface monitoring
rem.body: Continuous Shodan/OSINT sweep of exposed edge assets.

rem.title: Portal WAF & MFA
rem.body: Block defacement vectors; enforce MFA on admin panels.

killChain.scenarioTitle: Qilin ransomware via exposed VPN (finding C-01)
steps: Reconnaissance — Shodan/mass-scan identifies Rosneft VPN gateway exposing CVE-2026-50751
Initial access — affiliate exploits the VPN RCE to gain an unauthenticated foothold
Credential access — harvests domain credentials; disables/rotates around MFA gaps
Lateral movement — pivots from IT VPN toward trading systems and file servers
Collection & exfiltration — stages sensitive trading and HR data to a leak site
Impact — deploys Rust locker across ESXi and Windows; encrypts trading & backups
Extortion — double-extortion demand (€1M–€5M) with leak-site countdown

cbiqBridge.note: Trading downtime + leak-site extortion; highest EL scenario
